name: Tafsir al-Mizan
author: Allamah Muhammad Husayn Tabatabai
text:
# Ayahs

```arabic
وَقَالَ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ لَا تَسْمَعُوا۟ لِهَـٰذَا ٱلْقُرْءَانِ وَٱلْغَوْا۟ فِيهِ لَعَلَّكُمْ تَغْلِبُونَ (٢٦)
```

```english
The faithless say, ‘Do not listen to this Quran and hoot it down so that you may prevail [over the Apostle].’ (26)
```

```arabic
فَلَنُذِيقَنَّ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ عَذَابًا شَدِيدًا وَلَنَجْزِيَنَّهُمْ أَسْوَأَ ٱلَّذِى كَانُوا۟ يَعْمَلُونَ (٢٧)
```

```english
We will surely make the faithless taste a severe punishment, and We will surely requite them by the worst of what they used to do. (27)
```

```arabic
ذَٰلِكَ جَزَآءُ أَعْدَآءِ ٱللَّهِ ٱلنَّارُ ۖ لَهُمْ فِيهَا دَارُ ٱلْخُلْدِ ۖ جَزَآءًۢ بِمَا كَانُوا۟ بِـَٔايَـٰتِنَا يَجْحَدُونَ (٢٨)
```

```english
That is the requital of the enemies of Allah—the Fire! In it, they will have an everlasting abode, as a requital for their impugning Our signs. (28)
```

```arabic
وَقَالَ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ رَبَّنَآ أَرِنَا ٱلَّذَيْنِ أَضَلَّانَا مِنَ ٱلْجِنِّ وَٱلْإِنسِ نَجْعَلْهُمَا تَحْتَ أَقْدَامِنَا لِيَكُونَا مِنَ ٱلْأَسْفَلِينَ (٢٩)
```

```english
The faithless will say, ‘Our Lord! Show us those who led us astray from among jinn and humans so that we may trample them under our feet, so that they may be among the lowermost!’ (29)
```

```arabic
إِنَّ ٱلَّذِينَ قَالُوا۟ رَبُّنَا ٱللَّهُ ثُمَّ ٱسْتَقَـٰمُوا۟ تَتَنَزَّلُ عَلَيْهِمُ ٱلْمَلَـٰٓئِكَةُ أَلَّا تَخَافُوا۟ وَلَا تَحْزَنُوا۟ وَأَبْشِرُوا۟ بِٱلْجَنَّةِ ٱلَّتِى كُنتُمْ تُوعَدُونَ (٣٠)
```

```english
Indeed those who say, ‘Our Lord is Allah!’ and then remain steadfast, the angels descend upon them, [saying,] ‘Do not fear, nor be grieved! Receive the good news of the paradise, which you have been promised. (30)
```

```arabic
نَحْنُ أَوْلِيَآؤُكُمْ فِى ٱلْحَيَوٰةِ ٱلدُّنْيَا وَفِى ٱلْـَٔاخِرَةِ ۖ وَلَكُمْ فِيهَا مَا تَشْتَهِىٓ أَنفُسُكُمْ وَلَكُمْ فِيهَا مَا تَدَّعُونَ (٣١)
```

```english
We are your friends in the life of this world and in the Hereafter, and you will have in it whatever your souls desire, and you will have in it whatever you ask for, (31)
```

```arabic
نُزُلًا مِّنْ غَفُورٍ رَّحِيمٍ (٣٢)
```

```english
as a hospitality from One all-forgiving, all-merciful.’ (32)
```

```arabic
وَمَنْ أَحْسَنُ قَوْلًا مِّمَّن دَعَآ إِلَى ٱللَّهِ وَعَمِلَ صَـٰلِحًا وَقَالَ إِنَّنِى مِنَ ٱلْمُسْلِمِينَ (٣٣)
```

```english
Who has a better call than him who summons to Allah and acts righteously and says, ‘Indeed I am one of the Muslims’? (33)
```

```arabic
وَلَا تَسْتَوِى ٱلْحَسَنَةُ وَلَا ٱلسَّيِّئَةُ ۚ ٱدْفَعْ بِٱلَّتِى هِىَ أَحْسَنُ فَإِذَا ٱلَّذِى بَيْنَكَ وَبَيْنَهُۥ عَدَٰوَةٌ كَأَنَّهُۥ وَلِىٌّ حَمِيمٌ (٣٤)
```

```english
Good and evil [conduct] are not equal. Repel [evil] with what is best. [If you do so,] he between whom and you was enmity, will then be as though he were a sympathetic friend. (34)
```

```arabic
وَمَا يُلَقَّىٰهَآ إِلَّا ٱلَّذِينَ صَبَرُوا۟ وَمَا يُلَقَّىٰهَآ إِلَّا ذُو حَظٍّ عَظِيمٍ (٣٥)
```

```english
But none is granted it except those who are patient, and none is granted it except the greatly endowed. (35)
```

```arabic
وَإِمَّا يَنزَغَنَّكَ مِنَ ٱلشَّيْطَـٰنِ نَزْغٌ فَٱسْتَعِذْ بِٱللَّهِ ۖ إِنَّهُۥ هُوَ ٱلسَّمِيعُ ٱلْعَلِيمُ (٣٦)
```

```english
Should an incitement from Satan prompt you [to ill feeling], seek the protection of Allah. Indeed, He is the All-hearing, the All-knowing. (36)
```

```arabic
وَمِنْ ءَايَـٰتِهِ ٱلَّيْلُ وَٱلنَّهَارُ وَٱلشَّمْسُ وَٱلْقَمَرُ ۚ لَا تَسْجُدُوا۟ لِلشَّمْسِ وَلَا لِلْقَمَرِ وَٱسْجُدُوا۟ لِلَّهِ ٱلَّذِى خَلَقَهُنَّ إِن كُنتُمْ إِيَّاهُ تَعْبُدُونَ (٣٧)
```

```english
Among His signs are night and day, and the sun and the moon. Do not prostrate to the sun, nor to the moon, but prostrate to Allah who created them, if it is Him that you worship. (37)
```

```arabic
فَإِنِ ٱسْتَكْبَرُوا۟ فَٱلَّذِينَ عِندَ رَبِّكَ يُسَبِّحُونَ لَهُۥ بِٱلَّيْلِ وَٱلنَّهَارِ وَهُمْ لَا يَسْـَٔمُونَ ۩ (٣٨)
```

```english
But if they disdain [the worship of Allah], those who are near your Lord glorify Him night and day, and they are not wearied. (38)
```

```arabic
وَمِنْ ءَايَـٰتِهِۦٓ أَنَّكَ تَرَى ٱلْأَرْضَ خَـٰشِعَةً فَإِذَآ أَنزَلْنَا عَلَيْهَا ٱلْمَآءَ ٱهْتَزَّتْ وَرَبَتْ ۚ إِنَّ ٱلَّذِىٓ أَحْيَاهَا لَمُحْىِ ٱلْمَوْتَىٰٓ ۚ إِنَّهُۥ عَلَىٰ كُلِّ شَىْءٍ قَدِيرٌ (٣٩)
```

```english
Among His signs, is that you see the earth desolate; but when We send down water upon it, it stirs and swells. Indeed, He who revives it will also revive the dead. Indeed, He has power over all things. (39)
```

## (Commentary)

This is a return to the discourse regarding their disbelief in the Qur’an mentioned at the beginning of the Surah, and a mention of their plotting to invalidate its proof. Within these verses, there is a mention of the disbelievers (الكفار) and some of what follows from their misguidance, as well as the people of steadfastness among the believers (المؤمنين) and some of what awaits them in the Hereafter, along with other miscellaneous matters.

His saying, the Exalted: **وَقالَ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا لا تَسْمَعُوا لِهذَا الْقُرْآنِ وَالْغَوْا فِيهِ لَعَلَّكُمْ تَغْلِبُونَ** — "The faithless say, ‘Do not listen to this Quran and make a noise during its recitation, so that you may prevail.’" (41:26). Vain talk (اللغو) refers to an affair that has no basis, and in speech, it refers to that which has no meaning. It is said: *laghā yalghā* or *yalghū laghwan*, meaning he engaged in vain talk. The reference to the Qur’an while mentioning its name is evidence of their complete preoccupation with the Qur’an in order to efface its impact.

The verse indicates the ultimate extent of their inability to contend with the Qur’an by producing speech that equals or resembles it, or by establishing an argument that opposes it. This reached the point where they commanded one another not to listen to it and to produce vain speech during the Prophet’s (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله وسلم) recitation of the Qur’an, so that his recitation would be disrupted and its verses would not strike the ears of the people, thereby nullifying its effect—which is its prevailing power.

His saying, the Exalted: **فَلَنُذِيقَنَّ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا عَذاباً شَدِيداً** — "We will surely let the faithless taste a severe punishment..." (41:27). The *lām* is for an oath. What is meant by "those who disbelieve," according to the context of the verse, are those who said: "Do not listen to this Qur’an," even if the verse is general in its wording.

And His saying: **وَلَنَجْزِيَنَّهُمْ أَسْوَأَ الَّذِي كانُوا يَعْمَلُونَ** — "...and We will surely requite them by the worst of what they used to do." (41:27). It has been said: What is meant is the evil deed they used to perform, by stripping the superlative form (*af‘al*) of its comparative meaning. It has also been said: What is meant is the clarification of the recompense for the worst of their deeds, while remaining silent about the rest as an exaggeration in deterrence.

His saying, the Exalted: **ذلِكَ جَزاءُ أَعْداءِ اللهِ النَّارُ** — "That is the recompense of the enemies of Allah: the Fire..." (41:28). "That is the recompense" (ذلِكَ جَزاءُ) is a subject and predicate, and "the Fire" (النَّارُ) is an appositive (بدل) or an explanatory connective (عطف بيان) for "that," or the predicate of an omitted subject, the estimation being "it is the Fire," or a subject whose predicate is "they shall have therein the abode of eternity."

And His saying: **لَهُمْ فِيها دارُ الْخُلْدِ** — "...They will have in it the abode of eternity..." (41:28). Meaning, the Fire encompasses them all, but each of them has an abode within it specifically for them, remaining therein forever.

And His saying: **جَزاءً بِما كانُوا بِآياتِنا يَجْحَدُونَ** — "...as a recompense for their having impugned Our signs." (41:28). This is an absolute object (مفعول مطلق) for an omitted verb, the estimation being "they are recompensed with a recompense," or for the preceding verbal noun, namely His saying: "That is the recompense," similar to His saying: **فَإِنَّ جَهَنَّمَ جَزاؤُكُمْ جَزاءً مَوْفُوراً** — "...then indeed hell shall be your recompense, an ample recompense." (17:63).

His saying, the Exalted: **وَقالَ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا رَبَّنا أَرِنَا الَّذَيْنِ أَضَلَّانا مِنَ الْجِنِّ وَالْإِنْسِ** — "The faithless will say, ‘Our Lord! Show us the two who misled us from among the jinn and humans...’" (41:29). This is a reported statement they will utter while they are in the Fire. They ask Allah to show them those they followed from among the jinn and humans so they may place them under their feet to humiliate them and intensify their punishment, as suggested by their concluding statement: **نَجْعَلْهُما تَحْتَ أَقْدامِنا لِيَكُونا مِنَ الْأَسْفَلِينَ** — "...so that we may put them under our feet, so that they may be among the lowest.’" (41:29).

His saying, the Exalted: **إِنَّ الَّذِينَ قالُوا رَبُّنَا اللهُ ثُمَّ اسْتَقامُوا تَتَنَزَّلُ عَلَيْهِمُ الْمَلائِكَةُ** — "Indeed those who say, ‘Our Lord is Allah!’ and then remain steadfast, the angels descend upon them..." (41:30). Al-Raghib said: "Steadfastness (الاستقامة) is said of a path that is on a straight line, and the path of truth is likened to it, such as: **اهْدِنَا الصِّراطَ الْمُسْتَقِيمَ** — 'Guide us on the straight path.' He said: The steadfastness of a human being is his adherence to the straight methodology, such as His saying: 'Indeed those who say, ‘Our Lord is Allah!’ and then remain steadfast.'" [End quote]. In *al-Sihah*: "Steadfastness (الاستقامة) is moderation (الاعتدال); it is said: the matter became straight (استقام) for him." [End quote].

Thus, what is meant by His saying: **ثُمَّ اسْتَقامُوا** — "...and then remain steadfast" is adhering to the middle of the path without leaning or deviating, and remaining firm upon the statement they uttered. The Exalted said: **فَمَا اسْتَقامُوا لَكُمْ فَاسْتَقِيمُوا لَهُمْ** — "...So as long as they are true to you, be true to them." (9:7), and He said: **وَاسْتَقِمْ كَما أُمِرْتَ وَلا تَتَّبِعْ أَهْواءَهُمْ** — "...and be steadfast as you have been commanded, and do not follow their desires..." (42:15). The various interpretations narrated regarding this return to what has been mentioned.

This verse and what follows it are an exposition of the good state of the believers, just as the preceding verses were an exposition of the evil state of the disbelievers.

And His saying: **تَتَنَزَّلُ عَلَيْهِمُ الْمَلائِكَةُ أَلَّا تَخافُوا وَلا تَحْزَنُوا وَأَبْشِرُوا بِالْجَنَّةِ الَّتِي كُنْتُمْ تُوعَدُونَ** — "...the angels descend upon them [saying,] ‘Do not fear, nor be grieved; and receive the good news of the paradise which you were promised." (41:30). This is an announcement of how the angels will receive them by strengthening their hearts, comforting their souls, and giving them glad tidings of honor.

The angels secure them from fear (الخوف) and grief (الحزن). Fear only arises from an expected disliked matter, such as the punishment they fear or being deprived of Paradise which they dread. Grief only arises from a present disliked matter or an enduring evil, such as the sins they grieve over having committed or the good deeds they grieve over having missed. Thus, the angels comfort their souls that they are in security from fearing anything or grieving over anything; for their sins are forgiven and the punishment is averted from them.

Then they give them glad tidings of the promised Paradise by saying: "and receive the good news of the paradise which you were promised." In their saying: "which you were promised" (كُنْتُمْ تُوعَدُونَ), there is an indication that their descent with these glad tidings upon them occurs after the life of this world.

His saying, the Exalted: **نَحْنُ أَوْلِياؤُكُمْ فِي الْحَياةِ الدُّنْيا وَفِي الْآخِرَةِ** — "We are your friends in the life of this world and in the Hereafter..." (41:31). This is part of the completion of the glad tidings. Based on this, the mention of their guardianship (الولاية) over them in the life of this world—despite its time having passed—is by way of introduction and preparation for the mention of the Hereafter, to indicate that the guardianship of the Hereafter is a consequence of the guardianship of the world. It is as if it were said: We are your guardians in the Hereafter just as we were—since we were—your guardians in the life of this world, and we shall take charge of your affairs after this just as we took charge of them before.

The fact that the angels are guardians (أولياء) for them does not contradict the fact that the Exalted is the Guardian (الولي), because they are intermediaries of mercy and honor, having no authority of their own. Perhaps the mention of their guardianship over them in the verse, rather than His guardianship, is for the sake of contrast and comparison between the guardians of the Exalted and His enemies; for He said regarding His enemies: **وَقَيَّضْنا لَهُمْ قُرَناءَ** — "We have assigned for them companions..." (41:25), and He said regarding His guardians through the tongue of His angels: "We are your friends (أولياء)."

Through this contrast, it is understood that what is meant is their guardianship over them through guidance (التسديد) and support (التأييد); for the guiding angels are specifically for the people of Allah's guardianship. As for the guardian angels, those entrusted with provisions, lifespans, and others, they are common to both the believer and the disbeliever.

It has been said: The verse is from the speech of Allah, not the angels.

And His saying: **وَلَكُمْ فِيها ما تَشْتَهِي أَنْفُسُكُمْ وَلَكُمْ فِيها ما تَدَّعُونَ** — "...and you will have in it whatever your souls desire, and you will have in it whatever you ask for," (41:31). The pronoun "in it" (فِيها) in both places refers to the Hereafter. The origin of desire (الشهوة) is the soul's leaning, through one of its powers, toward what that power wants and finds pleasure in, such as the desire for food, drink, and marriage. The origin of claiming (الادعاء) — which is the *if‘ti‘āl* form of *du‘ā’* — is seeking (الطلب). Thus, the second sentence, namely His saying: "and you will have in it whatever you ask for," is broader in scope than the first, namely His saying: "you will have in it whatever your souls desire"; for desire is a specific seeking, while absolute seeking is more general than it.

Thus, the verse gives them glad tidings that they shall have in the Hereafter whatever their desires may attach to, such as eating, drinking, marriage, and other things—rather, what is broader than that and of a higher station, which is that they shall have whatever they wish therein, as the Exalted said: **لَهُمْ ما يَشاؤُنَ فِيها** — "They will have therein whatever they wish..." (50:35).

His saying, the Exalted: **وَمَنْ أَحْسَنُ قَوْلاً مِمَّنْ دَعا إِلَى اللهِ وَعَمِلَ صالِحاً وَقالَ إِنَّنِي مِنَ الْمُسْلِمِينَ** — "Who is better in speech than him who invites to Allah and acts righteously and says, ‘I am one of the Muslims’?" (41:33). The verse is connected to His previous saying: "The faithless say, ‘Do not listen to this Quran and make a noise during its recitation...’" (41:26). For they used to contend with the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله وسلم) just as they disputed the Qur’an. At the beginning of the Surah, their saying was mentioned: **قُلُوبُنا فِي أَكِنَّةٍ مِمَّا تَدْعُونا إِلَيْهِ** — "Our hearts are in veils from what you invite us to..." (41:5). So the Glorified supported His Prophet in this verse by stating that his speech—which is his invitation—is the best of speech.

Thus, His saying: "Who is better in speech than him who invites to Allah" refers to the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله وسلم), even if the wording of the verse encompasses everyone who invites to Allah. Since it is possible for an inviter to invite to Allah for a corrupt purpose—and an invitation of such a nature is not of the "best speech"—He qualified it with His saying: "and acts righteously" (وَعَمِلَ صالِحاً); for righteous deeds (العمل الصالح) reveal a righteous intention. However, righteous deeds do not necessarily reveal the true belief and commitment to it, and there is no goodness in speech that its speaker does not believe in. Therefore, He qualified it with His saying: "and says, ‘I am one of the Muslims’" (وَقالَ إِنَّنِي مِنَ الْمُسْلِمِينَ). What is meant by "saying" (القول) here is opinion and belief (الاعتقاد), as suggested by the context.

So when submission (الإسلام) to Allah and righteous deeds are completed for a person, and then he invites to Allah, his speech is the best of speech; because the best speech is the truest and most beneficial, and no speech is truer than the word of monotheism (التوحيد), nor more beneficial than it, as it is what guides man to the very core of his felicity.

His saying, the Exalted: **لا تَسْتَوِي الْحَسَنَةُ وَلَا السَّيِّئَةُ** — "Good and evil [conduct] are not equal..." (41:34). When He mentioned the best speech and that it is the invitation to Allah, and that the one truly established upon it is the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله وسلم), He turned to him with an exposition of the best path for the invitation and the one closest to the desired goal, which is influencing souls. So He addressed him saying: "They are not equal..." etc.

Thus, His saying: "Good and evil [conduct] are not equal" means the good trait and the evil trait are not equal in terms of their positive influence on souls. The "no" (لا) in "nor the evil" (وَلَا السَّيِّئَةُ) is extra to emphasize the negation.

And His saying: **ادْفَعْ بِالَّتِي هِيَ أَحْسَنُ** — "Repel [evil] with what is best..." (41:34). This is a new sentence in the sense of answering an implied question. It is as if the addressee, upon hearing His saying: "They are not equal," said: "So what should I do?" It was then said: "Repel..." etc. The meaning is: Repel with the trait that is best the evil trait that opposes and contradicts it. Thus, repel with the truth you possess their falsehood—not with another falsehood—and with your forbearance (الحلم) their ignorance, and with your forgiveness (العفو) their ill-treatment, and so on.

And His saying: **فَإِذَا الَّذِي بَيْنَكَ وَبَيْنَهُ عَداوَةٌ كَأَنَّهُ وَلِيٌّ حَمِيمٌ** — "...[and] behold, he between whom and you was enmity shall be as if he were a sympathetic friend." (41:34). This is an exposition of the effect of repelling with what is best and its result. The meaning is that if you repel with what is best, you will be surprised to find that your enemy has become as if he were a compassionate friend.

It has been said: "he between whom and you was enmity" (الَّذِي بَيْنَكَ وَبَيْنَهُ عَداوَةٌ) is more eloquent than "your enemy," which is why it was chosen over it despite its brevity.

Then Allah, the Glorified, magnified the act of repelling with what is best and praised it with the finest magnification and most profound praise by His saying: **وَما يُلَقَّاها إِلَّا الَّذِينَ صَبَرُوا وَما يُلَقَّاها إِلَّا ذُو حَظٍّ عَظِيمٍ** — "But none is granted it except those who are patient, and none is granted it except the greatly endowed." (41:35). Meaning, one who possesses an abundant portion of human perfection and virtuous traits.

In the verse, there is also a clear indication that the "great endowment" (الحظ العظيم) is found specifically for the people of patience (الصبر).

His saying, the Exalted: **وَإِمَّا يَنْزَغَنَّكَ مِنَ الشَّيْطانِ نَزْغٌ فَاسْتَعِذْ بِاللهِ إِنَّهُ هُوَ السَّمِيعُ الْعَلِيمُ** — "Should a temptation from Satan disturb you, then seek refuge in Allah; indeed He is the All-hearing, the All-knowing." (41:36). Prodding (النزغ) is a prick, which is poking the side or rear of a beast with a rod or similar object to incite it. The "if" (إِمَّا) in "should [a temptation] disturb you" (إِمَّا يَنْزَغَنَّكَ) is extra, and the original is "and if a temptation disturbs you, then seek refuge."

The prodders (النازغ) is Satan or his embellishment and whispering (الوسوسة). The first is more appropriate for the station of the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله وسلم), for Satan has no way to reach him through whispering. However, he can distort matters through whispering to those invited from among the people of disbelief and rejection, so they exaggerate their rejection, opposition, and harm toward him, such that repelling with what is best does not affect them. This leads to a prodding from Satan by intensifying the enmity between them, as in His saying: **مِنْ بَعْدِ أَنْ نَزَغَ الشَّيْطانُ بَيْنِي وَبَيْنَ إِخْوَتِي** — "...after Satan had caused a rift between me and my brothers..." (12:100). The Exalted said: **وَما أَرْسَلْنا مِنْ قَبْلِكَ مِنْ رَسُولٍ وَلا نَبِيٍّ إِلَّا إِذا تَمَنَّى أَلْقَى الشَّيْطانُ فِي أُمْنِيَّتِهِ** — "We did not send any apostle or prophet before you but that when he formed a wish Satan cast [something] into his wish..." (22:52).

If it is taken according to the second interpretation, it must be understood as a general instruction to complete the command, and it is, in a way, of the category of "I mean you, but listen, O neighbor."

And His saying: **فَاسْتَعِذْ بِاللهِ إِنَّهُ هُوَ السَّمِيعُ الْعَلِيمُ** — "...then seek refuge in Allah; indeed He is the All-hearing, the All-knowing." (41:36). Seeking refuge (العوذ), sanctuary (العياذ), shelter (المعاذ), and seeking protection (الاستعاذة) are all of the same meaning, which is taking recourse. The meaning is: Take recourse in Allah from his prodding; indeed He is the All-hearing of your request, the All-knowing of your state—or the All-hearing of your words, the All-knowing of your actions.

His saying, the Exalted: **وَمَنْ آياتِهِ اللَّيْلُ وَالنَّهارُ وَالشَّمْسُ وَالْقَمَرُ** — "Among His signs are the night and the day, and the sun and the moon..." (41:37). When the Glorified mentioned that his (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله وسلم) invitation is the best of speech and advised him to repel with the best of traits, He returned to the origin of the invitation, arguing for monotheism (الوحدانية) and the Resurrection (المعاد) in these three verses.

Thus, His saying: "Among His signs are the night and the day..." etc., is an argument based on the unity of governance (التدبير) and its continuity for the unity of the Governing Lord, and based on the unity of the Lord for the necessity of worshipping Him alone. Therefore, He followed it with His saying: **لا تَسْجُدُوا لِلشَّمْسِ وَلا لِلْقَمَرِ** — "...Do not prostrate to the sun, nor to the moon..." (41:37).

The discourse is in the sense of answering an implied question. It is as if when it was said: "Among His signs are the night and the day..." etc., and His unity in His Lordship was established, it was asked: "So what should we do?" It was then said: "Do not prostrate to the sun, nor to the moon," for they are two created and governed things from His creation; rather, single Him out for prostration and worship Him alone. Generally, the idolaters used to venerate the sun and the moon, even if none worshipped them except the Sabians, according to what has been said. The pronoun in "He created them" (خَلَقَهُنَ) refers to the night, the day, the sun, and the moon.

And His saying: **إِنْ كُنْتُمْ إِيَّاهُ تَعْبُدُونَ** — "...if it is Him that you worship." (41:37). Meaning, worshipping Him cannot coexist with worshipping others.

His saying, the Exalted: **فَإِنِ اسْتَكْبَرُوا فَالَّذِينَ عِنْدَ رَبِّكَ يُسَبِّحُونَ لَهُ بِاللَّيْلِ وَالنَّهارِ وَهُمْ لا يَسْأَمُونَ** — "But if they are disdainful, those who are with your Lord glorify Him night and day, and they do not tire." (41:38). Weariness (السأمة) is boredom. What is meant by "those who are with your Lord" (فَالَّذِينَ عِنْدَ رَبِّكَ) are the angels and the sincere ones among the servants of Allah. Discourse regarding this has already passed in the commentary on His saying: **إِنَّ الَّذِينَ عِنْدَ رَبِّكَ لا يَسْتَكْبِرُونَ عَنْ عِبادَتِهِ وَيُسَبِّحُونَهُ وَلَهُ يَسْجُدُونَ** — "Indeed those who are with your Lord are not too proud to worship Him: they glorify Him and prostrate to Him." (7:206).

And His saying: "they glorify Him" (يُسَبِّحُونَ لَهُ) and He did not say "they glorify Him" (يسبحونه) to indicate restriction and exclusivity, meaning they glorify Him specifically. And His saying: "night and day" (بِاللَّيْلِ وَالنَّهارِ) means constantly, without interruption, for the angels have neither night nor day.

The meaning is: If these disbelievers are too proud to prostrate to Allah alone, His worship is not removed from existence; for there are those who glorify Him with a constant glorification that does not cease, without weariness, and they are those who are with your Lord.

His saying, the Exalted: **وَمَنْ آياتِهِ أَنَّكَ تَرَى الْأَرْضَ خاشِعَةً** — "Among His signs is that you see the earth humble..." (41:39). Humility (الخشوع) is submissiveness. Quivering (الاهتزاز) is intense movement. Swelling (الربو) is growth, development, and rising. The quivering of the earth and its swelling is its movement with its vegetation and its rising.

In the verse, there is a representative metaphor (استعارة تمثيلية) in which the earth—in its barrenness and lack of vegetation, then its turning green and the growth and rising of its plants—is likened to a person who was of lowly state, in tattered clothes, submissive and humble, then obtained what sustains him, so he wore the finest clothes and stood up energetic and strutting, the radiance of bliss being recognized in his face.

The verse is intended as an argument for the Resurrection (المعاد), and the discussion of its content has been repeated in the preceding Surahs.

## (Narrative Discussion)

In *al-Majma‘*, regarding His saying, the Exalted: **أَرِنَا الَّذَيْنِ أَضَلَّانا** — "Show us the two who misled us..." (41:29): They mean Iblis of the devils—and Cain, the son of Adam, the first to innovate disobedience. This was narrated from Ali (عليه السلام).

I say: Perhaps this is of the nature of application (الجري), for the verse is general.

And in it, regarding His saying, the Exalted: **إِنَّ الَّذِينَ قالُوا رَبُّنَا اللهُ ثُمَّ اسْتَقامُوا** — "Indeed those who say, ‘Our Lord is Allah!’ and then remain steadfast..." (41:30): It is narrated from Anas that he said: "The Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله وسلم) recited this verse to us and then said: 'People have said it, then most of them disbelieved. So whoever says it until he dies has remained steadfast upon it.'"

And in it, regarding His saying, the Exalted: **تَتَنَزَّلُ عَلَيْهِمُ الْمَلائِكَةُ** — "...the angels descend upon them..." (41:30): Meaning at the time of death. Narrated from Mujahid and al-Suddi, and this was narrated from Abu Abdillah (عليه السلام).

In *Tafsir al-Qummi*, regarding His saying, the Exalted: **نَحْنُ أَوْلِياؤُكُمْ فِي الْحَياةِ الدُّنْيا** — "We are your friends in the life of this world..." (41:31): He said: "We used to guard you from the devils." **وَفِي الْآخِرَةِ** — "...and in the Hereafter," meaning at the time of death.

In *al-Majma‘*, regarding the verse, it was said: "We are your friends in the life of this world," meaning we guard you in the world and at the time of death in the Hereafter.

In *Tafsir al-Qummi*, regarding His saying, the Exalted: **ادْفَعْ بِالَّتِي هِيَ أَحْسَنُ** — "Repel [evil] with what is best..." (41:34): He said: "Repel the evil of one who mistreats you with your good deed—until he between whom and you was enmity becomes as if he were a sympathetic friend."
